TECHNICAL ANALYSIS

Basic Knowledge

Japanese Candlesticks

💡

I. Introduction

1. A Perennial War

You have to imagine candlesticks as a big endless war between two factions: the buyers (green, bullish) and the sellers (red, bearish) and where everyone fights to win the battle of profitability. When the forces of buyers and sellers are balanced, the battle is brought to a stalemate and neither side prevails. On the opposite, if one force dominates the other, we see a direction supported by volatility and volume.

We can also have fake movements that can give the illusion of predominance but end up in a fake-out. That's why, especially if you're a newcomer, you need to pay attention to closures..but what are closures?

In short, closures are a summary of the trading session. You can see them as a victory (bullish or bearish), depending on the timeframe you look at (1H-4H-1D-1W-1M).

Remember: the higher the timeframe, the lesser the noise, thus a better outlook on the market. That's why I personally prefer to pay attention to 1D-1W-1M since my approach is that of a trend rider. I'm not interested in the volatility that precedes the closures and this helps me analyze a potential trend better.

2. Candle structure

A Japanese candle is structured, essentially, with a so-called OHLC (Open-High-Low-Close) structure and it's a basic but crucial knowledge to master.

We have:

  • The body (Jittay in Japanese) that combines the opening price with the closing price and is represented by the widest, colored part of the candle.
  • The wicks (also called Kage or Shadows) that connect the maximum and minimum price reached, to the body with a line.
  • It’s where the price has spiked (up or down) before closing.

Thus, we have:

  • Upper shadows (Uwakage in Japanese)
  • Lower shadows (Shitakage in Japanese)

3. Candles strength

In the infographic I made here, you can see the strength of the candle, both for the bullish and the bearish case. A larger candle without shadows represents strong pressure, which is gradually decreasing as the shadows become more present.

This ties into another highly accurate concept: if there are more buyers than sellers, the buyers do not have anyone they can buy from. The price then increases until it becomes so high that the sellers once again find it attractive to get involved.

At the same time, the price is eventually too high for the buyers to keep buying. This concept applies of course also to the sellers, generating the requirements for a market to thrive.

II. Candlestick patterns, are they worth it?

In the world of trading, it’s easy to get caught up in the romance of candlestick patterns. I’m sure that, if you’re interested into this subject you already find out plenty of names like Doji, Hammer, Morning Star, Engulfing, and a long list of others passed around as if they hold some mystical key to market timing.

But let’s be clear, they don’t.

These names may feel familiar, even comforting (like if knowing the name would mean knowing what you’re talking about) but they’re largely unnecessary. In fact, they can become a crutch that stops you from developing real market awareness. The truth is simple: it’s not about what a candle is called, it’s about what it shows.

Every candle on a chart represents a struggle between buyers and sellers, that’s it. The open, high, low, and close are just the data points, but the story lies in the body, the space between the open and close. The size of that body is what truly matters because it reflects the pressure behind the move. A large candle body tells you that one side dominated with buyers overwhelming sellers, or vice versa. That pressure is what pushes price, not the shape or the label.

When you focus on names, you start looking for textbook patterns..a hammer here, a shooting star there and you begin trying to predict moves based on isolated shapes, but the market doesn’t care about your textbook. It moves because of conviction. For example, a “Bullish Engulfing” pattern means nothing if it forms without context. A “Doji” at the top of a rally isn’t a reversal signal unless you understand the shift in energy behind it. Naming candles is a shortcut, and like most shortcuts in trading, it often leads nowhere.

The hard truth is that candles only matter when you understand the intent behind them. A long-bodied bullish candle breaking through a level with momentum and volume speaks more than ten labeled patterns ever could. It shows that buyers stepped in with purpose, with strength and that’s what you want to see (if you’re bullish). That’s what tells you something useful.

Instead of memorizing dozens of patterns and their names, learn to read what the candle is saying. Is there force behind the move? Is the market hesitating? Is this candle rejecting a key level, or exploding through it? Are traders committing, or waiting? All of that can be seen in the size, speed, and placement of the candle, not in its name. When you stop labeling candles and start reading them as expressions of momentum and intent, everything shifts (and you’ll also filter a lot of random information) beginning to see the market as it is: a dynamic battlefield of pressure and reaction.

You stop looking for patterns to confirm your bias and start responding to what’s actually happening. In the end, candles don’t matter because of what they’re called but exclusively because of what they reveal. So forget dozen of names and focus on the pressure. As we will continue with the guide I will show you a 2 of them, the ones that I think are more relevant in the context of price action. (and this will also give you more but no too many information)

Marubozu

Marubozu is a candle that has no wicks (or very thin ones) meaning the price opened and closed at the extreme ends of the candle’s range. It shows strong and continuous buying or seling pressure throughout a session. What's important to understand in the context of a Marubozu paired with SMC? The “narrative” behind, which offers us a big clue.

A bullish marubozu following a sweep of sell-side liquidity (which is a SH) suggests that large buyers absorbed all available sell orders and aggressively pushed price upward without allowing sellers to regain control. This could be the beginning of a bullish displacement, where price breaks through previous highs, leaving behind a fair value gap. That imbalance, created by that strong move, generates inefficiency, which institutions often revisit later as part of the reaccumulation process.

In a bearish context, a marubozu after buy-side liquidity (which is a SL) is taken can confirm that the manufactured trap has been successful. The strong, unopposed move downward shows that the smart money have flipped the market structure and are now driving price in the opposite direction.

Engulfing

The engulfing pattern is very appealing in terms of price action because it shows that the previous force (buyers or sellers) are getting “wiped out” by the opposite force.

A bullish engulfing occurs when a small bearish candlestick is immediately followed by a much larger bullish candlestick whose body completely engulfs the body of the previous one. This formation suggests a potential shift in momentum from sellers to buyers.

The second candle must open below the close of the first and close above its open, demonstrating a complete overtaking of the previous session's price action, aka absorption.

The same concept applies in reverse for a bearish engulfing pattern, where a small bullish candle is followed by a large bearish one that fully covers it, hinting at growing selling pressure and the potential beginning of a downward movement.

These patterns are most effective when they appear after a well-established trend. It’s important not to place too much weight on engulfing formations that emerge at the very beginning of a trend or during periods of consolidation, as the context in which these patterns form is essential to their reliability.

In a mature trend where price has already shown consistent directional movement, an engulfing candle carries more weight, serving as a stronger signal that a reversal could be underway. This is because market participants are already committed to the prevailing direction, and a sudden engulfing pattern reflects a significant shift in sentiment and possibly the exhaustion of the current trend.

Interestingly, when a bearish engulfing pattern forms within an ongoing downtrend or a bullish engulfing appears in the middle of a strong uptrend, the meaning can be quite different. In these cases, the pattern may initially seem like a signal of reversal, but instead, it often reveals that opposing market forces attempted to counter the dominant trend and failed. Rather than indicating a trend change, these engulfing candles in the direction of the prevailing trend often serve as strong continuation signals. They show that the trend has encountered resistance or hesitation but ultimately re-confirmed its strength, brushing off the “challenge” and continuing in its original direction with renewed momentum.

Closures

Now that we've put it all together and you have a better understanding of candles, we can resume the initial topic. Remember when I was talking about the importance of closures? There is a big law here: if you're confused about the short timeframe, you need to extend it to have a clearer idea. This means that if the 1H chart confuses you, look at the 4H. If the 4H still confuses you, switch to the 1D, and so on.

I made a specific content diving deeper into this concept here : Untitled, make sure to check it out.

Technical application

Let's take an example with the daily chart near the Bitcoin 2021 top. Remember: each candle corresponds to a daily session of trading. What can we see? A clear narrative of shifting market sentiment, transitioning from bullish enthusiasm to growing bearish dominance.

Initially, PA breaks above the previous high (ATH) which was suggesting bullish continuation. However, the move lacked in conviction. There was no follow-through and the price swiftly reverses, indicating that the breakout was a false one or that buyers had exhausted their momentum at those levels.

This failed breakout was a subtle warning sign preceding a structural change. Following this, the market broke below the prior low and that break was decisive since we saw a strong bearish follow-through, signifying a change in control from buyers to sellers. It's the market's first clear signal that bullish structure has failed and that bears are beginning to assert dominance.

The following price movements attempted to push back up but failed to surpass the newly formed supply and the key high at 66,339$. This inability to create a new high reinforced the earlier shift and we can find more “sauce” to the recipe by plotting a trendline that connected lower highs, confirming persistent rejection at declining levels and giving the start to the bear market.

If we zoom out on the weekly timeframe instead, we can have an extended and clearer picture that shows, once again, how much being able to be patient is important.

You can in fact see that both weekly candles didn’t close above their highs and lows, but the 2nd weekly candle near the range low was still red and the 3rd one produced the impulse to the downside.

No clarity provided since the price doesn’t close above a high or below a key low? Good, I wait more for the price to give me a solid response. That's why it’s a crucial concept to understand: it can save your capital. Newcomers of course don't look at closures, and that's why they get trapped at the top of each bull market.

Conclusion

To conclude... what can we say about Japanese candlesticks? The truth is that all the confusion often created around names and specific formations is actually detrimental to learning and certainly not helpful for trading. What truly matters is the ability to read the context and recognize that a candlestick carries more significance when its body is large and extended, and much less when it has a pronounced wick since that, in itself, declares very little and can often be interpreted merely as a liquidity grab.

Relying blindly on the shape of a candlestick without considering where and when it appears often leads to misjudgments. The real meaning of a candle lies in its context: if it forms at a key level, near crucial zones of interest, or during a period of heightened volatility, then it may hold actual operational value. On the other hand, an isolated candle appearing outside of any structural logic is just a graphical element with little to no relevance.(fancy to highlight for many)

Moreover, it’s essential to pay close attention to the trading activity contained within the body of the candle. That’s where the real commitment between buyers and sellers takes place. A wide-bodied candle indicates decisive action and clear directional intent, evidence of real participation in the market. Conversely, a candle with a small body and long wicks may show indecision, manipulation, or simply noise, rather than a genuine shift in market sentiment.

Ultimately, what matters is not how a candle looks, but what it represents within the broader narrative of price. Understanding this is the first step toward cutting through educational noise and focusing on what truly makes the difference in the markets: reading the behavior of money and not patterns.
